content:
# Deuteronomy 28 — Disagreement Log
**SOPHIA** | EMET vs. LOGOS vs. PNEUMA | 68 verses (tochechah)

---

## V.1 — "Diligently listen" vs. "faithfully obey"

- **EMET:** "if you diligently listen to the voice of the LORD your God"
- **LOGOS:** "if you faithfully obey the voice of the LORD your God"
- **PNEUMA:** "If you fully obey the LORD your God"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "diligently listen" preserves the Hebrew infinitive absolute shamo'a tishma — "if you truly/diligently hear." (2) LOGOS: "faithfully obey" — imports faithfulness not explicit in the Hebrew. (3) PNEUMA: "fully obey" and drops "the voice of" — the Hebrew specifies qol (voice). **Resolution:** "if you diligently listen to the voice of the LORD your God" — EMET; preserves the idiom and "voice."

---

## V.1 — "Set you high above all the nations"

- **EMET:** "the LORD your God will set you high above all the nations of the earth"
- **LOGOS:** "the LORD your God will set you high above all the nations of the earth" (same)
- **PNEUMA:** "the LORD your God will set you high above all the nations on earth"

**Differences:** Minor: "of the earth" vs. "on earth." **Resolution:** "all the nations of the earth" — EMET/LOGOS.

---

## V.4 — Livestock terms

- **EMET:** "the offspring of your cattle and the young of your flock"
- **LOGOS:** "the increase of your herds and the young of your flock"
- **PNEUMA:** "the calves of your herds and the lambs of your flocks"

**Differences:** EMET: "offspring of your cattle" (shegar alafeicha); LOGOS: "increase of your herds"; PNEUMA: "calves of your herds." PNEUMA specifies "calves" and "lambs" — more specific than the Hebrew. **Resolution:** "the offspring of your cattle and the young of your flock" — EMET.

---

## V.5 — "Kneading bowl" vs. "kneading trough"

- **EMET/LOGOS:** "kneading bowl" (mishereth)
- **PNEUMA:** "kneading trough"

**Differences:** Minor lexical variation on mishereth. **Resolution:** "kneading bowl" — EMET/LOGOS; more established rendering.

---

## V.7 — Enemies "defeated" vs. "struck"

- **EMET:** "the LORD will cause the enemies who rise up against you to be struck before your face"
- **LOGOS:** "The LORD will cause your enemies who rise against you to be defeated before you"
- **PNEUMA:** "The LORD will grant that the enemies who rise up against you will be defeated before you"

**Differences:** EMET: "struck before your face" (nigafim lefaneicha); LOGOS/PNEUMA: "defeated before you." The Hebrew nigaf means struck/smitten (from nagaf), not simply "defeated." **Resolution:** "The LORD will cause your enemies who rise against you to be defeated before you" — LOGOS; "defeated" is idiomatic even though "struck" is more literal.

---

## V.7 — "One way... seven ways"

- **EMET:** "by one road they shall come out against you, and by seven roads they shall flee before you"
- **LOGOS:** "They shall come out against you one way and flee before you seven ways"
- **PNEUMA:** "They will come at you from one direction but flee from you in seven"

**Differences:** EMET: "by one road... by seven roads" (derekh echad... shiv'ah derachim); LOGOS: "one way... seven ways"; PNEUMA: "one direction... in seven." **Resolution:** "They shall come out against you one way and flee before you seven ways" — LOGOS.

---

## V.8 — "Storehouses" vs. "barns"

- **EMET:** "your storehouses" (be-asameicha)
- **LOGOS:** "your barns"
- **PNEUMA:** "your barns"

**Differences:** EMET: "storehouses" — the Hebrew asam is a storage facility, not necessarily a barn. LOGOS/PNEUMA: "barns." **Resolution:** "your storehouses" — EMET; more accurate to the Hebrew.

---

## V.10 — "In awe of you" vs. "afraid of you"

- **EMET:** "they shall be in awe of you"
- **LOGOS:** "they shall be afraid of you"
- **PNEUMA:** "they will stand in awe of you"

**Differences:** The Hebrew ve-yaru — from yare, which encompasses both "fear" and "reverence/awe." EMET/PNEUMA: "in awe"; LOGOS: "afraid." **Resolution:** "they shall be in awe of you" — EMET/PNEUMA; "in awe" better captures the combination of reverence and fear.

---

## V.11 — "Abound in good things" vs. "abound in prosperity"

- **EMET:** "the LORD will make you abound in good things" (le-tovah)
- **LOGOS:** "the LORD will make you abound in prosperity"
- **PNEUMA:** "The LORD will grant you abundant prosperity"

**Differences:** The Hebrew ve-hotirkha le-tovah means "and he will make you overflow for good." EMET: "abound in good things" — literal; LOGOS/PNEUMA: "prosperity." **Resolution:** "the LORD will make you abound in prosperity" — LOGOS; "prosperity" captures the agricultural and material fullness intended.

---

## V.12 — "Good treasury" vs. "good treasure" vs. "storehouse of his bounty"

- **EMET:** "his good treasure, the heavens"
- **LOGOS:** "his good treasury, the heavens"
- **PNEUMA:** "the heavens, the storehouse of his bounty"

**Differences:** The Hebrew otzaro ha-tov — "his good treasury/storehouse." EMET: "treasure"; LOGOS: "treasury"; PNEUMA: "storehouse of his bounty." PNEUMA reverses word order (heavens first). **Resolution:** "his good treasury, the heavens" — LOGOS.

---

## V.20 — me'erah, mehumah, migeret

- **EMET:** "the curse [me'erah], the panic, and the rebuke"
- **LOGOS:** "curses, confusion, and frustration"
- **PNEUMA:** "curses, confusion, and frustration"

**Differences:** EMET preserves the three Hebrew technical terms: me'erah (curse/blight), mehumah (panic/confusion), migeret (rebuke/destruction — possibly scribal variant; some read mig'eret). LOGOS/PNEUMA render all three functionally. **Resolution:** "the curse [me'erah], the panic, and the rebuke" — EMET; the technical vocabulary is significant in covenant context.

---

## V.21 — "Plague" vs. "pestilence"

- **EMET:** "the LORD will make the plague cling to you"
- **LOGOS:** "The LORD will make the pestilence cling to you"
- **PNEUMA:** "The LORD will plague you with diseases"

**Differences:** Hebrew ha-dever — the noun form "the pestilence/plague" (a specific disease). EMET/LOGOS preserve the noun form. PNEUMA uses the verb "plague." **Resolution:** "The LORD will make the pestilence cling to you" — LOGOS; "pestilence" is the precise term for dever.

---

## V.22 — cherev (sword/drought)

- **EMET:** "sword [drought]" with bracketed alternate
- **LOGOS:** "drought"
- **PNEUMA:** "drought" (and "scorching heat" for charavah elsewhere)

**Differences:** EMET preserves the textual ambiguity with the bracket. LOGOS/PNEUMA adopt "drought" for the agricultural context. **Resolution:** "drought" — follows LOGOS/PNEUMA; the agricultural plague context supports this reading. Textual note preserved in disagreement log.

---

## V.25 — "Horror" vs. "thing of horror" vs. "object of horror"

- **EMET:** "a thing of horror [za'avah] to all the kingdoms of the earth"
- **LOGOS:** "a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth"
- **PNEUMA:** "a thing of horror to all the kingdoms on earth"

**Differences:** EMET: "thing of horror" with [za'avah] gloss. LOGOS: "a horror." All capture the Hebrew za'avah (an object of trembling/horror). **Resolution:** "a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth" — LOGOS; concise and accurate.

---

## V.26 — "Carcass" vs. "dead body"

- **EMET:** "your carcass shall be food"
- **LOGOS:** "your dead body shall be food"
- **PNEUMA:** "Your carcasses will be food"

**Differences:** Hebrew nivlatekha — your corpse/carcass. PNEUMA pluralizes ("carcasses"). EMET: "carcass" (singular, as in the Hebrew). **Resolution:** "your carcass shall be food" — EMET; singular, as the Hebrew.

---

## V.27 — "Scurvy" vs. "scabs" vs. specific terms

- **EMET:** "scurvy [garav] and itch [cheres]"
- **LOGOS:** "scabs and itch"
- **PNEUMA:** "festering sores and the itch"

**Differences:** The Hebrew has garav (a skin disease — scab/itch) and cheres (itch/scurf). EMET provides Hebrew glosses. LOGOS: "scabs"; PNEUMA: "festering sores." **Resolution:** "scurvy and itch" — EMET; the bracketed Hebrew terms are noted; "scurvy" captures garav.

---

## V.29 — "Robbed" vs. "plundered"

- **EMET:** "oppressed and plundered all the days"
- **LOGOS:** "oppressed and robbed continually"
- **PNEUMA:** "day after day you will be oppressed and robbed"

**Differences:** "Plundered" (EMET) vs. "robbed" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for gazul. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "continually" vs. EMET's "all the days." **Resolution:** "oppressed and robbed continually" — LOGOS.

---

## V.30 — "Lie with her" vs. "ravish her" vs. "rape her"

- **EMET:** "another man shall lie with her"
- **LOGOS:** "another man shall ravish her"
- **PNEUMA:** "another man will take her and rape her"

**Differences:** The Hebrew yishkavennah — "shall lie with her" (the basic verb, not a violence-specific term). LOGOS: "ravish" adds a violence connotation. PNEUMA: "rape" is explicit. **Resolution:** "another man shall lie with her" — EMET; the Hebrew verb does not specify force; the violation is conveyed contextually.

---

## V.31 — "Save you" vs. "help you"

- **EMET:** "there shall be no one to save you"
- **LOGOS:** "there shall be no one to help you"
- **PNEUMA:** "no one will rescue them"

**Differences:** Hebrew v'ein moshia — "and there is no one saving/rescuing." EMET: "save"; LOGOS: "help"; PNEUMA: "rescue." All three render yasha differently. **Resolution:** "there shall be no one to help you" — LOGOS; "help" is idiomatic in this expression.

---

## V.32 — "Helpless" vs. "no strength" vs. "powerless"

- **EMET:** "there shall be no strength in your hand"
- **LOGOS:** "you shall be helpless"
- **PNEUMA:** "powerless to lift a hand"

**Differences:** Hebrew v'ein le-el yadkha — "and it is not in the power of your hand." EMET renders literally. LOGOS/PNEUMA are idiomatic. **Resolution:** "and there shall be no strength in your hand" — EMET; the idiom is vivid and worth preserving.

---

## V.36 — "King whom you set over yourself"

- **EMET:** "your king whom you set over yourself"
- **LOGOS:** "your king whom you set over you"
- **PNEUMA:** "the king you set over you"

**Differences:** EMET: "over yourself" for aleicha — more reflexive; LOGOS/PNEUMA: "over you." The Hebrew reflexive is worth preserving. **Resolution:** "your king whom you set over you" — LOGOS; clearer English.

---

## V.37 — "Taunt" vs. "byword"

- **EMET:** "a horror, a proverb, and a taunt"
- **LOGOS:** "a horror, a proverb, and a byword"
- **PNEUMA:** "a thing of horror, a byword, and an object of ridicule"

**Differences:** EMET: "taunt" (sheniah); LOGOS: "byword"; PNEUMA: "object of ridicule." The Hebrew sheniah means a byword/taunt. LOGOS: "byword" — the established translation. PNEUMA adds "object of ridicule" — paraphrase. **Resolution:** "a horror, a proverb, and a byword" — LOGOS.

---

## V.39 — "Work them" vs. "dress them" vs. "cultivate them" vs. "tend them"

- **EMET:** "You shall plant vineyards and work [them]"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall plant vineyards and dress them"
- **PNEUMA:** "You will plant vineyards and cultivate them"

**Differences:** The Hebrew ve-avadta — "you shall work/till them." LOGOS: "dress" (an archaic viticultural term). PNEUMA: "cultivate." **Resolution:** "You shall plant vineyards and tend them" — idiomatic.

---

## V.42 — tselatsal insect identification

- **EMET:** "the cicada [tselatsal]"
- **LOGOS:** "the cricket"
- **PNEUMA:** "Swarms of locusts"

**Differences:** The Hebrew tselatsal is an uncertain insect — possibly cicada (from the buzzing root tsalal), locust, or cricket. EMET: "cicada"; LOGOS: "cricket"; PNEUMA: "locusts" (and adds "swarms"). All three identifications are plausible. **Resolution:** "the locust" — the most familiar term; EMET's [tselatsal] gloss noted.

---

## V.46 — "Sign and wonder against you" vs. "sign and wonder to you"

- **EMET:** "upon you as a sign and as a wonder, and upon your offspring forever"
- **LOGOS:** "a sign and a wonder against you and your offspring forever"
- **PNEUMA:** "a sign and a wonder to you and your descendants forever"

**Differences:** LOGOS adds "against you" — interpretive. The Hebrew le-ot u-le-mofet — "for a sign and for a wonder" — is neutral as to direction. **Resolution:** "a sign and a wonder to you and your offspring forever" — PNEUMA; the neutral preposition is appropriate.

---

## V.47 — "Because of the abundance" — context of disobedience

- **EMET:** "because of the abundance of all things"
- **LOGOS:** "because of the abundance of all things"
- **PNEUMA:** "in the time of prosperity"

**Differences:** PNEUMA: "in the time of prosperity" — paraphrase of "because of the abundance of all things" (me-rov kol). **Resolution:** "because of the abundance of all things" — EMET/LOGOS.

---

## V.49 — "Nation from afar" and eagle simile

- **EMET:** "as the eagle swoops down [darting]"
- **LOGOS:** "swooping down like an eagle"
- **PNEUMA:** "like an eagle swooping down"

**Differences:** All three render ka'asher yid'eh ha-nesher — "as the eagle swoops." Minor word order variation. **Resolution:** "swooping down like an eagle" — LOGOS.

---

## V.50 — "Fierce of countenance" vs. "hard-faced" vs. "fierce-looking"

- **EMET:** "a nation fierce of countenance"
- **LOGOS:** "a hard-faced nation"
- **PNEUMA:** "a fierce-looking nation"

**Differences:** Hebrew az panim — "fierce/bold of face." EMET: "fierce of countenance" — literal. LOGOS: "hard-faced" — unusual English. PNEUMA: "fierce-looking." **Resolution:** "a fierce-looking nation" — PNEUMA; idiomatic.

---

## V.51 — "New wine" terminology

- **EMET:** "grain, new wine, or oil"
- **LOGOS:** "grain, wine, or oil"
- **PNEUMA:** "grain, new wine, or olive oil"

**Differences:** EMET/PNEUMA: "new wine" (tirosh — fresh/unfermented grape product); LOGOS: "wine." Tirosh is specifically new wine. **Resolution:** "grain, new wine, or oil" — EMET; tirosh is new wine, not aged wine.

---

## V.53 — Siege cannibalism description

- **EMET:** "you shall eat the fruit of your womb — the flesh of your sons and your daughters"
- **LOGOS:** "you shall eat the fruit of your womb, the flesh of your sons and daughters"
- **PNEUMA:** "you will eat the fruit of the womb — the flesh of the sons and daughters"

**Differences:** All three render the passage similarly. PNEUMA: "the fruit of the womb" vs. EMET/LOGOS: "the fruit of your womb" — minor pronoun variation. **Resolution:** "you shall eat the fruit of your womb — the flesh of your sons and your daughters" — EMET.

---

## V.54 — "Look grudgingly" vs. "begrudge" vs. "have no compassion"

- **EMET:** "shall look grudgingly upon his brother"
- **LOGOS:** "will begrudge food to his brother"
- **PNEUMA:** "will have no compassion on his own brother"

**Differences:** The Hebrew ve-takhas eino — "his eye shall look grudgingly/be mean toward." EMET: "look grudgingly" — literal of the eye idiom. LOGOS adds "food" (interpretive). PNEUMA: "have no compassion" — too far from the Hebrew eye/grudging idiom. **Resolution:** "shall look grudgingly upon his brother" — EMET.

---

## V.57 — Afterbirth description

- **EMET:** "her afterbirth that goes out from between her feet"
- **LOGOS:** "her afterbirth that comes out from between her feet"
- **PNEUMA:** "the afterbirth from her womb"

**Differences:** EMET/LOGOS: "from between her feet" — literal rendering of the Hebrew mi-bein ragleiha. PNEUMA: "from her womb" — sanitizes the graphic phrase. **Resolution:** "her afterbirth that comes out from between her feet" — LOGOS.

---

## V.58 — "This glorious and awesome name"

- **EMET:** "to fear this glorious and awesome name: the LORD your God"
- **LOGOS:** "that you may fear this glorious and awesome name, the LORD your God"
- **PNEUMA:** "do not revere this glorious and awesome name — the LORD your God"

**Differences:** EMET/LOGOS: "fear" (yir'ah); PNEUMA: "revere." The Hebrew liyre — to fear/revere. "Fear" is the more literal and theologically weighted term. **Resolution:** "that you may fear this glorious and awesome name — the LORD your God" — LOGOS.

---

## V.63 — "Rejoice" over destruction

- **EMET:** "so the LORD will rejoice over you to cause you to perish and to destroy you"
- **LOGOS:** "so the LORD will take delight in bringing ruin upon you and destroying you"
- **PNEUMA:** "so it will please him to ruin and destroy you"

**Differences:** The Hebrew ve-sas — "and the LORD will rejoice/take joy." EMET: "rejoice over you" — most literal. LOGOS: "take delight in bringing ruin" — softens the verb. PNEUMA: "please him" — weakest rendering of sus. **Resolution:** "so the LORD will rejoice over you to bring ruin upon you and to destroy you" — EMET's verb, LOGOS's clarity.

---

## V.65 — "Languishing soul" vs. "despairing heart"

- **EMET:** "a trembling heart and failing eyes and a languishing soul [nephesh]"
- **LOGOS:** "a trembling heart and failing eyes and a languishing soul"
- **PNEUMA:** "an anxious mind, eyes weary with longing, and a despairing heart"

**Differences:** PNEUMA reorders the triad and renders differently: "anxious mind" for lev ragaz (trembling heart), "weary with longing" for kalot einayim (failing eyes), "despairing heart" for nefesh deava (languishing soul). EMET/LOGOS preserve the Hebrew order. **Resolution:** "a trembling heart and failing eyes and a languishing soul" — EMET/LOGOS.

---

## V.66 — "Hang in doubt" vs. "hang in suspense" vs. "constant suspense"

- **EMET:** "your life shall hang in doubt before you"
- **LOGOS:** "your life shall hang in doubt before you" (same as EMET)
- **PNEUMA:** "You will live in constant suspense"

**Differences:** EMET/LOGOS: "hang in doubt" for talui (suspended/hanging). PNEUMA: "constant suspense" — paraphrase. **Resolution:** "your life shall hang in suspense before you" — blending EMET/LOGOS with slightly more idiomatic "suspense."

---

## V.67 — Night/day dread

- **EMET:** "you shall be in dread night and day, and you shall have no assurance of your life"
- **LOGOS:** "Night and day you shall be in dread and have no assurance of your life"
- **PNEUMA:** "never sure of your life"

**Differences:** PNEUMA: "never sure of your life" for "have no assurance of your life." All three convey the same meaning; PNEUMA is more idiomatic. **Resolution:** "and you shall be in dread night and day, and you shall have no assurance of your life" — EMET.

---

## V.68 — "Bring you back... in ships"

- **EMET:** "the LORD will bring you back to Mitsrayim in ships, by the road of which I said to you: You shall not see it again"
- **LOGOS:** "the LORD will bring you back in ships to Egypt, a journey that I promised that you should never make again"
- **PNEUMA:** "The LORD will send you back in ships to Egypt on a journey I said you should never make again"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "Mitsrayim" (Hebrew name); LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Egypt." Project convention uses Hebrew names for EMET but English forms for consensus. (2) LOGOS: "a journey that I promised" — interpretive. EMET: "by the road of which I said to you: You shall not see it again" — more literal. **Resolution:** "the LORD will bring you back to Egypt in ships, by the road of which I said to you: You shall never see it again" — blending EMET and LOGOS.

---

*Total disagreements logged: 36*
*Arbiter: SOPHIA*
